patterns.name: Cognitive Apprenticeship
patterns.summary: A teacher makes their own expert thinking visible while a learner works alongside them — not just watching the finished product, but watching the reasoning that produces it. The method runs through six moves: the expert models the cognitive process aloud, coaches the learner's attempts with targeted feedback, and scaffolds with just enough support to keep the task within reach. Then the expert gradually releases control as the learner articulates their own reasoning, reflects on how it compares to expert practice, and finally explores on their own. What separates cognitive apprenticeship from plain demonstration is that the expert's thinking is never hidden. Every heuristic, every wrong turn, every diagnostic question is spoken out loud rather than smoothed into a polished performance.
patterns.intent: Make expert thinking visible during practice so that learners acquire both skill and the cognitive strategies that produce it, not just the surface behavior.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a learner needs to acquire not just a procedure but the judgment that guides its application — reading for understanding, mathematical problem-solving, writing, diagnosis, debugging, or any complex cognitive task where the visible product conceals the reasoning that created it. Use when a learner is stuck not because they lack knowledge but because they lack a model of how an expert actually thinks through the same problem. Also applicable when an autonomous agent is being trained on tasks that require multi-step reasoning under uncertainty, and the trainer can expose the reasoning chain rather than just the correct output.

inputs.name: Expert practitioner willing to think aloud
inputs.description: A trainer who can externalize their own cognitive process during task performance — naming heuristics, noticing dead ends, and narrating diagnostic reasoning rather than presenting polished output.

inputs.name: Authentic task in the target domain
inputs.description: A real or realistic problem from the domain the learner needs to master — complex enough to require genuine expert reasoning but bounded enough to complete in a teaching session.

inputs.name: Learner with access to the task environment
inputs.description: A learner who can observe the expert's performance and then attempt the same task, receiving coaching on their own cognitive process rather than just correction of outcomes.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has internalized not just the task steps but the metacognitive strategies — when to use which heuristic, how to diagnose a stuck point, when to backtrack — that the expert brought to the task.

outputs.name: Articulated expert reasoning model
outputs.description: A made-explicit account of how expert thinking actually runs in the domain — the masterpiece — which the learner can compare their own process against and use as a navigation aid in future independent practice.

steps.name: Modeling
steps.description: The expert performs the target task while thinking aloud, making every cognitive move visible: the heuristics applied, the dead ends noticed, the sub-goals chosen. The learner observes and builds a conceptual model of what expert performance actually looks like from the inside.

steps.name: Coaching
steps.description: The learner attempts the task while the expert observes. The expert offers targeted hints, reminders, and feedback that address the learner's current cognitive bottleneck. The goal is not to correct output after the fact but to intervene in the reasoning process while it is happening.

steps.name: Scaffolding
steps.description: The expert provides supports — worked subproblems, prompting questions, partial solutions — that let the learner handle the parts of the task they cannot yet manage alone. This keeps the overall task at the edge of the learner's reach rather than breaking it into smaller safe problems.

steps.name: Articulation
steps.description: The learner is asked to make their own reasoning visible — explaining what they just did, why they chose a particular move, how they diagnosed a problem. Articulation forces the learner to become aware of processes they may have been executing without noticing.

steps.name: Reflection
steps.description: The learner compares their own problem-solving process — now made visible through articulation — against the expert's model. The comparison surfaces gaps the learner did not know existed and reframes stuck points as skill-buildable rather than talent-fixed.

steps.name: Exploration
steps.description: The learner is set a task with no expert present, pushing them to apply the cognitive strategies they have practiced in a genuinely self-directed way. Exploration is where the internalized model becomes operational. The learner discovers what they can and cannot yet do without a scaffold.

principles: Expert thinking must be visible, not performed — a polished demonstration teaches output, not process. Cognitive apprenticeship requires the trainer to expose heuristics, dead ends, and uncertainty.
Scaffolding collapses toward zero — the goal is to remove supports as fast as competence allows. Permanent scaffolding is not apprenticeship, it is dependency.
Articulation before reflection — a learner who cannot describe their own process cannot compare it against anything. Articulation is the prerequisite for the reflection step to have content.
patterns.threshold: The learner can attempt the target task independently, narrate their own reasoning while doing so, and identify — unprompted — where their approach diverges from the expert model they observed.
patterns.masterpiece: A session log or narrated recording where the expert's think-aloud model is visible alongside the learner's subsequent articulation of their own process, with the reflection step showing the learner identifying at least one gap they had not previously named.

known_uses.name: Collins, Brown, Newman — Reciprocal Teaching (Palincsar and Brown, 1984)
known_uses.quote: Modeling involves an expert's performing a task so that the students can observe and build a conceptual model.
known_uses.note: The canonical worked example in Collins, Brown, Newman 1989 is Palincsar and Brown's reciprocal teaching program, which applied modeling, coaching, and scaffolding to reading comprehension in schools.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of the expert showing only correct performance rather than genuine thinking, including dead ends. A smooth demonstration teaches the learner what success looks like; it does not teach them how to navigate toward it.

The anti-pattern of maintaining scaffolding beyond the point where the learner can manage without it. A learner who is always coached never reaches the exploration phase and cannot perform independently under real conditions.

The anti-pattern of jumping from coaching to reflection without requiring the learner to make their own reasoning visible first. Without articulation, reflection compares the expert's made-explicit model against the learner's still-implicit process — the comparison cannot surface real gaps.

sources.title: Cognitive Apprenticeship: Teaching the Crafts of Reading, Writing, and Mathematics (Collins, Brown, Newman, 1989)
sources.quote: In apprenticeship, the processes of the activity are visible. In schooling, the processes of thinking are often invisible to both the students and the teacher.

sources.title: Cognitive Apprenticeship: Making Thinking Visible (Collins, Brown, Holum, 1991)
sources.quote: Reflection involves enabling students to compare their own problem-solving processes with those of an expert, another student, and ultimately, an internal cognitive model.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Expert models task
thinking aloud — all heuristics visible] --> B[Learner observes
builds conceptual model]
    B --> C[Learner attempts task
expert coaches in real time]
    C --> D[Expert scaffolds
just enough support]
    D --> E[Learner articulates
own reasoning process]
    E --> F[Learner reflects
compares own process to expert model]
    F --> G{Gap identified?}
    G -- Yes --> C
    G -- No --> H[Learner explores
independently, no expert]
    H --> I[More capable learner
cognitive strategies internalized]

patterns.example_scenario: A senior software engineer is teaching a junior developer how to debug a production incident — not by fixing it themselves, but by narrating every step: 'I see a spike in latency around 14:03, so my first hypothesis is a slow query — let me check the slow query log before I look at anything else. Nothing there. So I'm now thinking connection pool exhaustion — here's how I check that.' The junior watches, then takes the keyboard while the engineer watches and asks guiding questions: 'What are you thinking is the most likely cause right now?' After the session, the junior explains their own diagnostic process out loud. The engineer points out that the junior jumped to code changes before exhausting infrastructure hypotheses — a gap in diagnostic ordering the junior did not know they had. This visible gap, surfaced through articulation and reflection, is exactly what cognitive apprenticeship produces that solo practice cannot.

patterns.name: Cohort-Based Learning
patterns.summary: A bounded group of learners starts, progresses through, and completes a program together — experiencing the same milestones at the same time, which turns the peer group itself into a learning resource. The shared timeline creates accountability structures that self-paced formats cannot produce: a learner who falls behind has real people waiting for them, not just a progress bar. Cohort peers accumulate shared context across sessions — they know each other's prior work, blind spots, and thinking styles — which makes peer feedback progressively sharper as the cohort matures. The pattern has roots in medical and law school residency cohorts. It was explicitly named and theorized as a distinct online pedagogical design by Wes Kao and Gagan Biyani around the altMBA program (Seth Godin, 2014) and the Maven platform.
patterns.intent: Make the peer group a primary learning resource by synchronizing progress so learners share context, accountability, and feedback quality that deepens as the cohort matures.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the target skill requires feedback from people who share the same learning context — not from strangers who lack shared reference points — and when real-time peer accountability improves completion over self-paced formats. Use for professional development, domain-specific skills, and any program where the social network of fellow learners is itself a lasting outcome. Avoid for purely knowledge-transfer goals where individual pacing has no cost.

inputs.name: Defined cohort of learners with shared goals
inputs.description: A bounded group — typically 15 to 50 people — who begin the program at the same time and share a domain, role, or ambition that gives them enough common ground to make peer feedback meaningful.

inputs.name: Time-bounded curriculum with synchronous touchpoints
inputs.description: A program with a fixed start and end date, including regular synchronous sessions (live calls, workshops, or structured peer review cycles) that require all cohort members to show up at the same time.

inputs.name: Peer review and accountability mechanisms
inputs.description: Structured ways for cohort members to review each other's work, give feedback, and notice when a peer is falling behind — not just consume content in parallel.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has acquired the target skill with feedback calibrated by people who watched the same journey — richer than feedback from an anonymous reviewer and more honest than feedback from a solo observer.

outputs.name: Cohort peer network
outputs.description: A lasting professional network of people who share domain, context, and the history of working through the same hard problems together — the masterpiece — which persists long past program completion as a career-shaping resource.

steps.name: Define cohort composition and shared baseline
steps.description: Recruit a cohort whose members share enough domain or goal alignment that peer feedback will be meaningful from day one. A cohort of strangers with incompatible goals produces generic feedback. Shared starting context is the input that makes the cohort valuable as a learning resource.

steps.name: Anchor the cohort with synchronous milestones
steps.description: Schedule regular synchronous sessions — live critiques, shared retrospectives, or cohort-wide deliverable reviews — that require all members to be at the same point in the program. These sessions keep the cohort synchronized and prevent asynchronous drift into self-paced isolation.

steps.name: Build structured peer accountability
steps.description: Pair or small-group learners for regular peer review and accountability check-ins. Accountability is not just social pressure — it is the mechanism that keeps feedback loops tight and prevents the silent dropout that plagues self-paced formats.

steps.name: Harvest cohort context as curriculum deepens
steps.description: Design later sessions to explicitly use earlier cohort work as material — referencing a peer's prior submission, comparing solutions from different cohort members, building on shared failures from week two. This harvesting is what makes cohort feedback progressively sharper rather than remaining generic throughout.

principles: Shared timeline is the mechanism, not a constraint — the synchrony that looks inflexible is exactly what produces the accountability and shared context that make peer feedback valuable.

The network is a lasting deliverable — a well-run cohort produces not just skill but a professional community that outlives the program. Design for the network's durability, not just the curriculum's completion.
patterns.threshold: Cohort members give each other feedback that references shared program history — citing a peer's earlier work, comparing against a cohort norm — rather than feedback a stranger could have given. Completion rate exceeds typical self-paced formats for the same content.
patterns.masterpiece: A peer network of former cohort members who continue to exchange work, opportunities, and feedback after program completion — the durable social capital the cohort format uniquely produces.

known_uses.name: altMBA — Seth Godin (2014)
known_uses.quote: Cohorts are unique in that they allow students to engage in ongoing academic, professional, and social journeys together.
known_uses.note: The altMBA (2014) and subsequently the Maven platform (Wes Kao and Gagan Biyani) are credited with popularizing the explicit cohort-based course design for online professional education.

known_uses.name: Medical residency programs — established cohort model
known_uses.quote: Placing students with similar interests/goals in the same cohort allowed us to bring our individual experiences to a common area where we were trying to develop, which exposed us to different types of thinking.
known_uses.note: Medical residency cohorts are the long-established institutional antecedent; the pattern was later theorized and named as a distinct online pedagogical design.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of calling a self-paced course a cohort by starting everyone on the same date but running no synchronous touchpoints. Without shared milestones, the cohort is a mailing list — members have no reason to stay in sync and the accountability mechanism never activates.

The anti-pattern of peer review that produces feedback a stranger could have given — because the cohort was not designed to accumulate shared context. Peer feedback is only a learning resource when peers know each other's prior work.

sources.title: Cohort-based learning to transform learning in graduate public health (Frontiers in Education, 2024)
sources.quote: Cohorts are unique in that they allow students to engage in ongoing academic, professional, and social journeys together.

sources.title: Wes Kao — Cohort-based courses articles
sources.quote: Wes Kao and her co-founder Gagan Biyani at Maven coined the term 'cohort based courses.'

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Cohort recruited
shared domain + goals] --> B[Program begins
all members in sync]
    B --> C[Synchronous milestone
live session / shared deliverable]
    C --> D[Structured peer review
feedback on shared context]
    D --> E{Program complete?}
    E -- No --> C
    E -- Yes --> F[Cohort network formed
persists post-program]
    F --> G[More capable learner
+ durable peer network]

patterns.example_scenario: A 6-week online program for product managers runs with a cohort of 25 participants who all start on the same Monday. Each week has a synchronous live workshop on Tuesday evening and a peer review cycle where every participant submits a one-page product brief for two cohort members to critique. By week four, peer feedback is noticeably sharper: reviewers reference a submitter's week-two brief when commenting on week-four choices, compare approaches across cohort members, and catch contradictions that a fresh reader would miss. A participant who goes quiet for five days receives a direct message from their accountability partner, not an automated nudge. At week six, 22 of 25 participants complete the capstone project — a completion rate that historical self-paced versions of the same curriculum never reached. Two years later, several cohort members are still trading job leads and draft documents in a private group chat that has no official connection to the program.

patterns.name: Pair Programming
patterns.summary: Two developers share one machine and one keyboard — one drives (writes code), the other navigates (observes, thinks one step ahead, catches errors). The roles switch regularly. Pair programming is one of the twelve core practices of Extreme Programming (Beck, 1999) and the practice that gives XP its most visible identity. As a learning pattern, pairing transfers both explicit knowledge (how to use a library, how to structure a query) and tacit knowledge (how an experienced developer reads an error trace, where they instinctively reach first) through direct side-by-side observation. This is the same mechanism as cognitive apprenticeship but embedded in production work rather than teaching sessions. Pairs produce fewer defects — not because either programmer is smarter, but because the navigator catches errors the driver misses in real time.
patterns.intent: Accelerate skill transfer and reduce defect rate by placing two practitioners at one workstation — making the experienced practitioner's reasoning visible to the less experienced one in the context of real production work.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a junior practitioner needs to absorb the reasoning patterns of a senior one faster than code review alone can deliver, or when a task is complex enough that a second set of eyes in real time — not after the fact — reduces the cost of errors. Especially effective for onboarding, for debugging complex production problems, and for tasks where the cost of a defect escaping to review is high. Also applicable as a training technique for autonomous agents that can take a turn as navigator — observing human reasoning in code and asking clarifying questions.

inputs.name: Two practitioners with a shared task
inputs.description: A driver and a navigator who share a machine, a keyboard, and a real task — not a practice problem — with enough complexity that both roles are genuinely engaged rather than one person watching the other work alone.

inputs.name: Role-switching discipline
inputs.description: A commitment to swap driver and navigator roles on a regular cadence — every 20–30 minutes at minimum — so that the learning benefit is not one-directional and the navigator does not mentally disengage.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A less experienced practitioner who has absorbed reasoning patterns — diagnostic habits, error-reading heuristics, design instincts — that they could not acquire from reading code or attending code review.

outputs.name: Shared codebase with lower defect rate
outputs.description: Working code produced by the pair — the masterpiece — which is demonstrably more robust than code produced by either practitioner alone, because the navigator catches errors in real time before they compound.

steps.name: Establish roles and task
steps.description: Agree which person drives first and which navigates. Define the task clearly enough that both roles know what done looks like — vague tasks produce aimless pairing where the navigator has nothing to navigate toward.

steps.name: Driver codes, navigator thinks ahead
steps.description: The driver implements, narrating their choices out loud when possible. The navigator watches not just the current line but the next problem — catching typos, noting edge cases, thinking about the next design decision — without interrupting the driver's flow for minor issues.

steps.name: Navigator intervenes at decision points
steps.description: The navigator speaks up when they see an error about to compound, a design choice about to go the wrong way, or a heuristic being applied in the wrong context. Intervention is not correction after the fact — it is redirection before the cost is paid.

steps.name: Switch roles on schedule
steps.description: At a fixed interval, swap keyboard. The switch is non-negotiable — if only the more experienced practitioner drives, the less experienced one never builds the production coding muscle the pattern is supposed to develop. Role-switching is what makes the learning bidirectional.

principles: Two minds on one task is not half the speed — the navigator's real-time error catching typically outweighs the coordination overhead, especially on complex or defect-sensitive work.
The navigator must be genuinely engaged, not watching — a disengaged navigator produces no learning benefit and no error catching. The role requires active anticipation, not passive observation.
patterns.threshold: After a pairing session, the less experienced practitioner can explain — not just recall — at least one reasoning pattern they observed the more experienced practitioner apply, and can identify where they would apply it themselves in a new context.
patterns.masterpiece: A working production artifact produced by the pair, accompanied by the less experienced practitioner's narrated account of what they learned from the experienced practitioner's reasoning during the session — demonstrating that the tacit knowledge transfer happened, not just the task completion.

known_uses.name: Extreme Programming (Kent Beck, 1999)
known_uses.org: First introduced in the C3 (Chrysler Comprehensive Compensation) project
known_uses.quote: Pair programming consists of two programmers sharing a single workstation (one screen, keyboard, and mouse among the pair).
known_uses.note: Pair programming is one of the twelve core practices of XP as defined by Kent Beck in Extreme Programming Explained (1999). The Agile Alliance traces the earliest written account to the article 'Chrysler goes to Extremes.'

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of the navigator mentally checking out — browsing, thinking about other tasks — while the driver codes. A passive navigator produces no error catching and no learning; it is one person working while another watches.

The anti-pattern of the more experienced practitioner monopolizing the driver role because it is faster, leaving the less experienced practitioner as a permanent observer. Without driving, the junior practitioner never builds the production coding muscle the pattern is supposed to develop.

The anti-pattern of applying pair programming to tasks simple enough for either practitioner to complete alone without any meaningful decision points. Pairing on trivial tasks produces overhead without the defect-catching or knowledge-transfer benefit.

sources.title: Pair Programming — Agile Alliance Glossary
sources.quote: Pair programming consists of two programmers sharing a single workstation (one screen, keyboard, and mouse among the pair). The programmer at the keyboard is usually called the 'driver', the other, also actively involved in the programming task but focusing more on overall direction is the 'navigator'.

sources.title: Extreme Programming Explained (Kent Beck, 1999)
sources.quote: All production code is written with two people looking at one machine, with one keyboard and one mouse.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Two practitioners
one task, one machine] --> B[Driver codes
narrates choices aloud]
    B --> C[Navigator thinks ahead
catches errors before they compound]
    C --> D{Decision point
or error spotted?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Navigator intervenes
redirects before cost is paid]
    D -- No --> F{Role switch
time?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Swap keyboard
roles reverse]
    G --> B
    F -- No --> B
    E --> B
    B --> H[Task complete
lower defect rate + tacit knowledge transferred]
    H --> I[More capable learner
+ shared robust artifact]

patterns.example_scenario: A senior backend engineer is paired with a junior who has been writing code for 18 months but has never debugged a race condition. The task is real: a production intermittent failure in an order processing queue. The senior drives first, narrating: 'I see intermittent, so my first hypothesis is timing — I'm looking for shared mutable state before I touch anything else.' The junior watches, then takes the keyboard after 25 minutes. The senior notices the junior reaching for a log statement before checking the queue consumer configuration: 'Before you log, what are we actually trying to confirm? Let's name the hypothesis first.' This single intervention — naming the hypothesis before instrumenting — is a diagnostic habit the junior could not have acquired from a code review comment on a fixed bug. After the session, the junior can explain the hypothesis-first diagnostic pattern and can name where they would apply it in a different codebase.

patterns.name: Peer Instruction
patterns.summary: The instructor pauses the lecture to pose a short conceptual question — a ConcepTest — that requires genuine understanding rather than recall. Learners think individually and commit to an answer (often by show of hands or clicker), then turn to a neighbor and argue for their answer, trying to convince each other. The instructor then polls again and explains. Invented by Eric Mazur at Harvard in 1991 after discovering that his physics students could pass standard tests while holding fundamental misconceptions about Newtonian mechanics. The key insight is that a student who understood a concept 15 minutes ago explains it more effectively to someone who does not yet understand it than a professor who understood it 30 years ago. The recent understander still remembers the specific confusion they had to resolve.
patterns.intent: Replace passive absorption of lectures with active sense-making by requiring learners to commit to an answer, argue for it with a peer, and update their understanding before moving on.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply in any lecture or large-group teaching context where the learner population is heterogeneous — some ahead, some behind — and where conceptual misconceptions (not just knowledge gaps) are the primary barrier to understanding. Particularly effective in STEM courses where learners can do calculations correctly while holding wrong physical intuitions. Also applicable to AI training pipelines: an agent prompted to explain a concept to a simulated peer, then receive a counter-explanation, performs better than one that only receives correct answers.

inputs.name: ConcepTest — a diagnostic conceptual question
inputs.description: A short multiple-choice question designed to surface a specific known misconception — not a recall test, but a question that requires genuine understanding to answer correctly. The wrong answer choices must reflect the actual wrong intuitions learners typically hold.

inputs.name: Heterogeneous group of learners
inputs.description: A group where some learners have understood the concept and some have not — the productive condition for peer instruction, because the recent understanders are the effective peer teachers.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has actively resolved a specific conceptual confusion through the act of arguing their position with a peer — not just heard the right answer, but worked through why it is right and why the wrong answer is wrong.

outputs.name: Visible learning state
outputs.description: A real-time diagnostic read of where the class is — the masterpiece — surfaced by the vote distributions before and after peer discussion, which tells the instructor which misconceptions are widespread and which are already resolved.

steps.name: Pose the ConcepTest and require individual commitment
steps.description: Present the question and ask every learner to think individually and commit to an answer before any discussion. The individual commitment step is essential: it forces each learner to formulate a position rather than free-riding on a neighbor's answer. Skip this step and the peer discussion becomes a social conformity exercise, not a reasoning one.

steps.name: First vote — reveal distribution
steps.description: Collect and display the distribution of answers (by clicker, hand raise, or anonymous poll). The distribution is informative for both instructor and learners: if more than 70% already have the right answer, move on; if fewer than 30% have it, the concept needs direct instruction first; if 30–70% have it, the conditions for productive peer discussion exist.

steps.name: Peer discussion — turn to your neighbor
steps.description: Ask learners to find a neighbor who answered differently and try to convince each other. The instruction is to argue, not just share answers: 'Try to convince your neighbor that your answer is correct.' This is the generative step — articulating a position forces the articulator to discover gaps in their own reasoning.

steps.name: Second vote and explanation
steps.description: Poll again and compare the distribution before and after discussion. Then explain the correct answer, explicitly addressing the specific wrong intuitions the most popular wrong answers reveal. The explanation now lands differently because learners have already committed to a position and felt the friction of a counter-argument.

principles: Individual commitment before discussion — without a prior individual answer, peer discussion becomes social information-gathering rather than reasoning. The commitment is what makes the subsequent argument productive.

The recent understander is the more effective teacher — a student who resolved a misconception 10 minutes ago remembers the path through the confusion better than an expert who resolved it years ago. Peer instruction leverages this structural advantage.
patterns.threshold: Second-vote correct-answer rates are consistently higher than first-vote rates for the same questions, and learners can explain why the most popular wrong answer is wrong — not just which answer is right.
patterns.masterpiece: A semester-long record of ConcepTest vote distributions (pre- and post-discussion) showing systematic comprehension gains, alongside a curriculum of ConcepTests calibrated to the class's actual misconceptions — a diagnostic and instructional tool in one.

known_uses.name: Eric Mazur — Harvard Physics (1991–present)
known_uses.quote: Lectures are interspersed with short, conceptual questions called ConcepTests. Students formulate individual answers to these questions and then discuss their answers with neighbors, trying to convince each other, before hearing the instructor's explanation.
known_uses.note: Mazur developed peer instruction after seeing that his Harvard physics students passed standard tests while holding Newtonian misconceptions — as measured by the Force Concept Inventory developed by Halloun and Hestenes (1985).

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of writing questions that test memory rather than conceptual understanding — questions where the right answer is retrievable without genuine comprehension. A recall question produces no productive peer discussion because the understander and the non-understander both just need to remember, not reason.

The anti-pattern of skipping the individual commitment step and moving directly to peer discussion. Without a prior individual answer, learners adopt a neighbor's position through social conformity rather than updating through reasoning — the mechanism that produces learning is bypassed.

The anti-pattern of not running the second vote after peer discussion, removing the diagnostic read that tells the instructor whether discussion actually moved the class and which misconceptions persist.

sources.title: Peer Instruction: A User's Manual (Mazur, 1997)
sources.quote: Lectures are interspersed with short, conceptual questions called ConcepTests. Students formulate individual answers to these questions and then discuss their answers with neighbors, trying to convince each other, before hearing the instructor's explanation.

sources.title: Peer Instruction: Ten Years of Experience and Results (Crouch and Mazur, 2001)
sources.quote: Turn to your neighbor — the classic catch-phrase of PI methodology, whereby teachers encourage students to think about a question, vote on their answer, and then turn to their neighbor to engage, rather than sitting passively in a lecture.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Instructor poses ConcepTest
diagnostic conceptual question] --> B[Individual think time
every learner commits to answer]
    B --> C[First vote
reveal distribution]
    C --> D{30–70% correct?}
    D -- Below 30% --> E[Direct instruction first
then retry]
    D -- Above 70% --> F[Move on
concept understood]
    D -- Yes: 30-70% --> G[Peer discussion
turn to neighbor, argue position]
    G --> H[Second vote
reveal new distribution]
    H --> I[Instructor explains
addresses specific wrong intuitions]
    I --> J[More capable learner
misconception resolved through argument]

patterns.example_scenario: A physics instructor asks: 'A heavy ball and a light ball are dropped from the same height in a vacuum. Which hits the ground first?' After individual think time, the first vote shows 60% chose 'the heavy ball' (a classic Aristotelian misconception) and 40% chose 'they hit at the same time.' The instructor asks learners to turn to a neighbor who answered differently and argue. In 90 seconds, the room is loud. The second vote shows 85% now correctly choose 'they hit at the same time.' The instructor explains — but now the explanation lands differently: every learner who changed their answer has already gone through the specific argument that broke their intuition. The explanation is a confirmation of something they just reasoned through rather than a fact handed to them. The learner who switched from 'heavy ball' to 'same time' because their neighbor argued convincingly is far less likely to revert to the misconception than one who simply heard the right answer stated.

patterns.name: Community of Practice
patterns.summary: A group of people who care about the same thing — a domain — interact regularly, help each other, and develop a shared way of doing and talking about that thing over time. Coined by Jean Lave and Etienne Wenger in their 1991 book Situated Learning, the concept started as an observation about how apprentices actually learn: not primarily from formal instruction but from increasing participation in a community of practitioners who are doing the real work. Wenger formalized the three-element definition in 1998: a community of practice has a shared domain (the topic it cares about), a community (the members who engage with each other), and a practice (the shared repertoire of approaches, tools, stories, and vocabulary the community develops and refines). The core pedagogical claim is that learning is not transfer of information into heads. It is becoming a more capable participant in a community that produces valuable work.
patterns.intent: Enable learning through increasing participation in a community of practitioners, so that newcomers develop competence by doing real work alongside more experienced members rather than through formal instruction alone.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the target competence is tacit enough that it cannot be fully specified in curriculum — when what it means to be good at the craft is partly held in the community's practices, vocabulary, and stories rather than in any single person's explicit knowledge. Works for professional communities, open-source contributor networks, craft guilds, research groups, and any domain where mastery means belonging to a community of practitioners, not just holding a certificate. Also applicable to autonomous agent populations that develop shared behavioral conventions through repeated interaction on shared tasks.

inputs.name: Shared domain with genuine stakes
inputs.description: A topic or practice area that community members actually care about advancing — not an organizational designation, but something members would pursue even without institutional mandate. Domains with genuine stakes produce communities that sustain themselves.

inputs.name: Mix of newcomers and experienced practitioners
inputs.description: The productive condition for learning through participation: newcomers start at the periphery and take on more consequential work as their competence grows, while experienced members model and narrate what good practice looks like in real situations.

inputs.name: Regular interaction on real problems
inputs.description: Opportunities for members to work on real domain problems together — not just share information — so that the community's shared repertoire develops through actual practice rather than through curated content.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A practitioner who has moved from peripheral to fuller participation in the community — able to contribute work that other members recognize as competent, and to narrate their reasoning in the community's vocabulary.

outputs.name: Community's shared repertoire
outputs.description: The evolved set of practices, vocabulary, tools, and stories the community develops through sustained interaction — the masterpiece — which is the community's collective knowledge asset and the primary vehicle through which it transmits competence to newcomers.

steps.name: Enter the community at the periphery
steps.description: A newcomer begins with legitimate but low-stakes tasks — ones that are real (not practice problems) but peripheral enough that errors do not compromise the community's core work. This legitimate peripheral participation is the mechanism: the newcomer is doing real work, not being trained, but the work is calibrated to their current competence.

steps.name: Participate in the community's real work
steps.description: The newcomer takes on progressively more central tasks as competence develops, observing more experienced members in the process. The learning is embedded in the doing, not separated from it as a training phase.

steps.name: Develop the shared repertoire
steps.description: The community collectively develops and maintains its shared repertoire — the vocabulary, tools, approaches, and stories that encode the community's practical knowledge. Newcomers acquire this repertoire through participation, not instruction. They start by using the vocabulary before they fully understand it and develop understanding through use.

steps.name: Become a more central participant over time
steps.description: As a learner's competence grows, they move from periphery to core — eventually becoming one of the practitioners who models and narrates good work for the next generation of newcomers. The community reproduces its competence through this continuous cycle, not through a fixed curriculum.

principles: Learning is becoming, not receiving — competence is measured by growing participation in the community, not by absorbing content. The community is the curriculum.
Peripheral participation must be legitimate — newcomers must do real work, not practice problems. The 'peripheral' refers to the task's centrality, not its authenticity.
patterns.threshold: A newcomer can complete a real community task and have the output recognized as competent by more experienced community members — without requiring remediation or special accommodation.
patterns.masterpiece: A community whose shared repertoire — vocabulary, tools, approaches, canonical stories — is actively used and evolved by its members, and through which newcomers demonstrably develop competence faster than they would in isolation or formal training alone.

known_uses.name: Lave and Wenger — Situated Learning (1991)
known_uses.org: University of California, Berkeley; University of California, Irvine
known_uses.quote: Learning viewed as situated activity has as its central defining characteristic a process that we call legitimate peripheral participation.
known_uses.note: Lave and Wenger observed apprenticeship learning in multiple craft communities (Vai and Gola tailors, naval quartermasters, meat cutters, midwives, AA members) and found that learning was embedded in participation rather than in formal instruction.

known_uses.name: Wenger — Communities of Practice (1998)
known_uses.quote: Communities of practice are groups of people who share a concern or a passion for something they do and learn how to do it better as they interact regularly.
known_uses.note: Wenger's 1998 book Communities of Practice: Learning, Meaning, and Identity formalized the three-element model (domain, community, practice) and extended the concept from craft apprenticeship to organizational and professional settings.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of forming a group with organizational mandate but no genuine shared domain — members attend meetings without caring about advancing a common practice. Without real domain stakes, the shared repertoire never develops and newcomers have nothing to participate toward.

The anti-pattern of assigning newcomers practice problems rather than real peripheral tasks. Practice problems do not produce the same learning as real work because they carry no community stakes — the newcomer is not yet participating, they are preparing to participate, which is a structurally different experience.

The anti-pattern of a community that is excellent at producing work but has no legible path from periphery to core — newcomers cannot see how to take on more central tasks and either stay peripheral indefinitely or leave. The community's competence does not reproduce.

sources.title: Situated Learning: Legitimate Peripheral Participation (Lave and Wenger, 1991)
sources.quote: Learning viewed as situated activity has as its central defining characteristic a process that we call legitimate peripheral participation.

sources.title: Communities of Practice: Learning, Meaning, and Identity (Wenger, 1998)
sources.quote: Communities of practice are groups of people who share a concern or a passion for something they do and learn how to do it better as they interact regularly.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Newcomer enters community
at the periphery] --> B[Legitimate peripheral participation
real but low-stakes tasks]
    B --> C[Observes more experienced
practitioners on central work]
    C --> D[Takes on more central tasks
as competence grows]
    D --> E[Acquires shared repertoire
vocabulary, tools, stories]
    E --> F{Full participant?}
    F -- Not yet --> D
    F -- Yes --> G[Becomes practitioner
models for next newcomer]
    G --> H[Community reproduces
competence through participation]

patterns.example_scenario: A junior data scientist joins an open-source machine learning library. Her first contribution is a documentation fix — legitimate, real, merged into the main branch, but peripheral to the library's core algorithmic work. She watches pull request discussions between senior maintainers, absorbing their vocabulary: 'numerically stable,' 'memory layout matters here,' 'this is a footgun for new users.' After three months she submits a small bug fix in a utility module, with a test. A senior maintainer reviews it and pushes back on the test coverage — a specific feedback pattern the junior had never received in her day job. She revises. Six months in, she takes on a feature proposal, has it debated in a GitHub issue thread, and ships it after two rounds of revision. Two years in, she is reviewing other people's pull requests, using the same vocabulary she first encountered in discussion threads, catching the same classes of issues the senior maintainers caught in her early work. She did not receive formal instruction in any of this — she learned by participating in increasingly central work in a community that cared about getting it right.

patterns.name: Mentorship and Coaching
patterns.summary: A more experienced practitioner (mentor or coach) engages one-to-one with a less experienced learner in a sustained relationship aimed at developing the learner's capability and judgment. Mentoring and coaching are related but distinct: mentors primarily share their own experience and wisdom, building the relationship over months or years to develop the whole practitioner; coaches primarily use questioning and reflection to draw out the learner's own capability, focusing on specific performance areas over a shorter horizon. Both patterns are older than formal education — mentoring takes its name from Mentor, the figure entrusted with Odysseus's son in Homer's Odyssey — but were formalized as distinct pedagogical approaches in the organizational literature of the 1980s and 1990s (Clutterbuck, Parsloe, Whitmore). The pattern also applies to autonomous agent training: an agent that receives sustained, individualized feedback from a human or agent trainer develops faster than one that receives only batch correction.
patterns.intent: Accelerate a learner's development of judgment, not just skill, through a sustained one-to-one relationship where the more experienced party provides individualized guidance unavailable in group or self-directed formats.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the learner needs to develop judgment — knowing when and how to apply skill in novel situations — rather than just acquiring procedures. Mentoring is most effective for long-arc career development and navigating domain-specific wisdom that is not codified anywhere. Coaching is most effective for specific performance gaps where the learner has latent capability that targeted reflection can unlock. Use when no group format can provide the individualization the learner needs. Also appropriate for autonomous agents being developed by human trainers who track the agent's specific failure modes over time rather than providing generic feedback.

inputs.name: Committed mentor or coach with relevant experience
inputs.description: A practitioner who has navigated the domain or challenge the learner faces, is willing to invest sustained time in one-to-one engagement, and can tailor their approach to the specific learner's profile rather than delivering a generic curriculum.

inputs.name: Learner with identified development goals
inputs.description: A learner who can name what they are trying to develop — specific enough to make progress visible, broad enough to require more than a tutorial. Undirected mentorship diffuses into general conversation without development.

inputs.name: Sustained engagement over time
inputs.description: A commitment to multiple sessions over weeks or months — not a one-time consultation. The relationship develops the mentor's or coach's model of the specific learner, which is what makes individualized guidance possible.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A practitioner who has developed judgment calibrated to their specific context — not just acquired a skill list, but built the decision-making model that determines when and how to apply skills in situations the curriculum did not cover.

outputs.name: Individualized development trajectory
outputs.description: A documented or tacit account — the masterpiece — of the learner's specific growth arc: where they started, what their particular obstacles were, what specific interventions moved them, and where they are now. This individualized account is what the mentor or coach uniquely provides.

steps.name: Establish the development goal and baseline
steps.description: At the start of the relationship, the mentor or coach and the learner name what the learner is trying to develop and what the current baseline looks like. Vague goals ('become a better leader') produce vague development. Specific ones ('learn to run difficult stakeholder conversations without losing the room') produce legible progress.

steps.name: Provide individualized guidance on real situations
steps.description: The core of the relationship: the mentor or coach responds to real situations the learner is navigating — not textbook cases. The mentor shares how they handled similar situations and why; the coach asks questions that surface the learner's own reasoning and expose where it breaks down.

steps.name: Build the learner's self-observation capacity
steps.description: Both mentoring and coaching aim to develop the learner's ability to observe and adjust their own practice — not to create dependency on the mentor or coach. The relationship is working when the learner begins to ask themselves the questions the mentor or coach used to ask.

steps.name: Progressively reduce guidance as judgment develops
steps.description: A good mentor or coach tracks the learner's growing capacity and reduces direction as it grows — shifting from providing answers to asking questions, then from asking questions to debriefing the learner's own decisions. The relationship succeeds when it becomes unnecessary.

principles: Individualization is the mechanism — a mentor or coach who delivers the same guidance to every learner is a curriculum, not a mentor. The value is in the tailored response to this specific person's specific situation.

The goal is independence, not reliance — a mentoring or coaching relationship that produces a learner who still needs the mentor to make decisions has failed. The masterpiece is a learner who has internalized the mentor's better questions.
patterns.threshold: The learner can navigate a novel difficult situation in the domain — one they have not encountered before — and produce a post-action account that shows they applied the judgment the relationship was designed to develop, without needing to consult the mentor or coach first.
patterns.masterpiece: A learner whose decision-making in the domain has visibly changed — tracked across real situations over the course of the relationship — in ways attributable to the individualized guidance rather than to experience alone.

known_uses.name: Eric Parsloe — Coaching, Mentoring and Assessing (1992)
known_uses.org: Oxford School of Coaching and Mentoring
known_uses.quote: Coaching is a process that enables learning and development to occur and thus performance to improve.
known_uses.note: Parsloe's 1992 work was among the first to formally distinguish coaching from mentoring as complementary but distinct pedagogical approaches in professional development.

known_uses.name: David Clutterbuck — European developmental mentoring model
known_uses.quote: The European, development-based model of mentoring emphasizes the mentor's experience and wisdom to enable the mentee to become self-reliant and to take ownership of their personal and professional development.
known_uses.note: Clutterbuck distinguished the European developmental mentoring model (which emphasizes learner autonomy) from the US sponsorship model (which emphasizes mentor power), establishing that the goal of mentoring is the learner's independence.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of a mentoring relationship that produces a learner who routes all decisions through the mentor rather than developing their own judgment. A mentor who provides answers rather than developing the learner's capacity to generate better answers is creating a dependency, not a more capable practitioner.

The anti-pattern of regular mentoring sessions without named development goals, which drift into general conversation and career networking. Without legible goals, neither the mentor nor the learner can tell whether the relationship is producing development.

The anti-pattern of a coach who delivers the same intervention framework to every learner regardless of their specific profile. Coaching that is not individualized to the specific learner's reasoning patterns and failure modes produces generic development indistinguishable from a group training program.

sources.title: Coaching, Mentoring and Assessing (Eric Parsloe, 1992)
sources.quote: Coaching is a process that enables learning and development to occur and thus performance to improve.

sources.title: Coaching and Mentoring Factsheet (CIPD)
sources.quote: Coaching and mentoring are development approaches based on the use of one-to-one conversations to enhance an individual's skills, knowledge or work performance.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Development goal established
baseline named] --> B[Learner navigates real situation]
    B --> C{Mentor or coach?}
    C -- Mentor --> D[Shares own experience
why they chose what they chose]
    C -- Coach --> E[Asks questions
surfaces learner's own reasoning]
    D --> F[Learner reflects
updates judgment]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Judgment
developing?}
    G -- Not yet --> B
    G -- Yes --> H[Reduce guidance
learner self-observes]
    H --> I[More capable learner
independent judgment in domain]

patterns.example_scenario: A mid-career product manager is struggling to run executive stakeholder reviews without losing the room — decisions get relitigated, sponsors check out, and nothing gets shipped. Her coach does not give her a stakeholder management framework. Instead, after each review, they debrief: 'Walk me through the moment when you felt the room shift. What had just happened? What were you tracking when it happened?' Over six sessions, a pattern emerges: the PM is providing context before stating the decision, which gives opponents time to organize objections before hearing what is being decided. The coach names it. The PM revises her structure — decision first, context second. The next review goes differently. Three months into the coaching relationship, the PM is running pre-mortems on her own presentations, asking herself the same questions the coach used to ask, and no longer needing the debriefs to catch the pattern. The masterpiece is not the framework — it is the PM's acquired capacity to observe her own practice and adjust in real time.

patterns.name: Learning by Teaching
patterns.summary: The learner teaches the material to someone else — a peer, a student, or even an imagined audience — and in doing so discovers the gaps in their own understanding that passive study concealed. The pedagogical insight is ancient: Seneca wrote 'while we teach, we learn' (docendo discimus) in the 1st century CE. Jean-Pol Martin formalized it as a systematic classroom method (Lernen durch Lehren, LdL) in German schools in the 1980s, and Richard Feynman popularized it as a personal study technique. The protege effect — the finding that preparing to teach material produces stronger retention and understanding than preparing for a personal test on the same material — is well-supported empirically. The mechanism: teaching requires the learner to build an explanation that works for a specific other person. This forces them to surface and resolve their own incomplete understanding rather than paper over it with recognition memory.
patterns.intent: Surface and resolve gaps in a learner's understanding by requiring them to teach the material to another person, because the act of constructing an explanation reveals what the learner does not yet know.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply after an initial learning phase — when the learner believes they understand the material — as a test and deepener of that understanding. Use when passive review or re-reading would produce false confidence: the learner can recognize correct answers without being able to generate correct explanations. Especially effective for conceptual material where understanding is built out of connected ideas rather than isolated facts, because the gaps in connected understanding surface only when the learner must produce a coherent explanation rather than retrieve a stored answer. Also applicable to autonomous agents: an agent prompted to explain a concept to a simulated novice, then receive a follow-up question, surfaces reasoning gaps faster than one that only verifies correct answers.

inputs.name: A learner who has studied the material once
inputs.description: Someone who believes they understand the topic — the false confidence that learning-by-teaching is designed to test and break.

inputs.name: A real or simulated audience
inputs.description: A peer who genuinely does not understand the material, a student in a structured setting, or — in the Feynman technique — an imagined child audience. The audience must be specific enough that the learner calibrates their explanation to a real person rather than producing a generic monologue.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner whose understanding has been deepened by the act of teaching — who has resolved the gaps that passive study concealed and can now generate explanations rather than only recognize correct ones.

outputs.name: Explanation that reveals mastery
outputs.description: The teacher-learner's explanation of the concept — the masterpiece — which demonstrates the quality of their understanding more accurately than any test score. A clear, gap-free explanation of a complex topic to a naive audience is the strongest evidence of genuine mastery.

steps.name: Study the material to the point of believing you understand it
steps.description: Complete initial learning — reading, lecture, practice problems. The important step is reaching the point of apparent understanding. Learning-by-teaching only works as a deepener and gap-finder; it is not a substitute for initial engagement with the material.

steps.name: Attempt to teach it without notes
steps.description: Explain the concept to the real or imagined audience using only what you can retrieve and generate — not what you can recognize when prompted. The constraint of no notes is what forces the gaps to surface: a learner who 'understands' a concept but cannot explain it without referring back to the source does not yet understand it.

steps.name: Locate and name the gaps
steps.description: When the explanation stalls, becomes circular, or cannot answer a follow-up question from the audience, that is a gap. Name it explicitly: 'I cannot explain why this is the case, only that it is.' Named gaps are learnable; vague discomfort is not.

steps.name: Return to the source to fill the gap and re-teach
steps.description: Go back to the material to resolve the named gap. Then re-attempt the explanation — not from the beginning but from the gap. Repeat until the explanation is gap-free. Each iteration of gap-finding and filling deepens the understanding layer that passive study leaves thin.

principles: Teaching exposes what recognition conceals — a learner who can recognize a correct answer has lower-quality understanding than one who can generate a correct explanation. Teaching forces the test that recognition hides.

Gaps must be named to be filled — a learner who notices their explanation is weak but does not name specifically where it breaks down will re-read the material and re-arrive at the same false confidence.
patterns.threshold: The learner can explain the concept to a naive audience without stalling, without circular reasoning, and without referring to notes — and can answer follow-up questions that probe the concept's edge cases.
patterns.masterpiece: A clear, gap-free explanation of the target concept delivered to a real or simulated naive audience — demonstrable by the audience's ability to apply the concept correctly in a new situation based solely on the teacher-learner's explanation.

known_uses.name: Jean-Pol Martin — Lernen durch Lehren (LdL), German schools, 1982–
known_uses.quote: In the early 1980s, Jean-Pol Martin systematically developed the concept of having students teach other in the context of learning French as a foreign language.
known_uses.note: Martin's 1985 dissertation formalized LdL and in 1987 he founded a network of over a thousand teachers implementing the method across subjects.

known_uses.name: Richard Feynman — Feynman Technique (personal study method)
known_uses.quote: The Feynman Technique asks you to explain a concept in simple language as if teaching it to someone with no background.
known_uses.note: The Feynman Technique operationalizes learning-by-teaching as a four-step individual study method: choose a concept, explain it plainly, identify gaps, simplify further.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of producing a fluent-sounding explanation that skips over the hard parts — the learner narrates the parts they understand and elides the parts they do not, producing an apparently complete explanation with unexamined holes. The mechanism requires actively hunting for gaps, not performing competence.

The anti-pattern of finding a gap but returning to passive re-reading rather than targeted resolution of the specific named gap. A learner who re-reads the whole chapter after finding one gap is doing avoidance learning — they will re-emerge with the same false confidence about the parts they already understood and the same gap about the part they did not.

sources.title: Learning by Teaching — Wikipedia (Jean-Pol Martin, LdL, Feynman Technique)
sources.quote: In the early 1980s, Jean-Pol Martin systematically developed the concept of having students teach other in the context of learning French as a foreign language.

sources.title: The Feynman Technique: How to Learn Anything by Teaching It (Glasp)
sources.quote: The Feynman Technique asks you to explain a concept in simple language as if teaching it to someone with no background.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Learner studies material
reaches apparent understanding] --> B[Attempts to teach
without notes to real or imagined audience]
    B --> C{Explanation
stalls or becomes circular?}
    C -- No --> D[Audience asks follow-up
edge case question]
    D --> E{Answer available?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Explanation complete
masterpiece: gap-free account]
    E -- No: gap found --> G[Name the gap explicitly]
    C -- Yes: gap found --> G
    G --> H[Return to source
target only the named gap]
    H --> B
    F --> I[More capable learner
genuine understanding replaces recognition]

patterns.example_scenario: A data analyst has read three articles about causal inference and believes she understands it. She attempts to explain it to a colleague who works in marketing with no statistics background. She gets through correlation-is-not-causation without trouble. She starts explaining instrumental variables and stalls: she can say 'an instrument is a variable correlated with the treatment but not the outcome' but when her colleague asks 'why does that help us establish causation?' she finds she cannot answer. She notices the gap, names it — 'I understand the definition but not the mechanism' — and goes back to one paper to find the specific answer. She returns, explains it again, this time getting through the mechanism. Her colleague asks: 'So when would you actually use this in practice?' She stalls again. Another named gap. After two iterations, she can explain instrumental variables clearly enough that her colleague can correctly identify whether a proposed analysis in a meeting would require one. The masterpiece is not her understanding — it is the colleague's ability to apply the concept, which would not exist if the analyst had not been forced to produce a gap-free explanation.

patterns.name: Jigsaw Classroom
patterns.summary: Students are divided into home groups, with each member assigned a different piece of the overall topic. Each goes to an expert group — everyone assigned the same piece — to study and master it, then returns to the home group to teach their piece to the others. Like a jigsaw puzzle, no single student has the full picture, so every piece — and every student who holds it — is essential for the group to understand the whole. Invented by Elliot Aronson and colleagues in Austin, Texas in 1971 as an urgent response to racial tension in newly desegregated schools. The structural feature that made it work was the elimination of competition: each student had information the others needed, so cooperation was the rational strategy rather than a moral exhortation.
patterns.intent: Distribute knowledge across learners such that every person's expertise is genuinely necessary for the group's understanding, making cooperation the rational strategy and peer teaching the primary learning mechanism.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a topic can be genuinely partitioned into pieces of comparable depth that together form the whole, and when reducing intra-group competition is a design goal alongside knowledge coverage. Use when the learner population includes members who tend to be passive in traditional group work — the jigsaw structure makes passivity structurally costly to the group, not just socially uncomfortable. Less effective for topics that cannot be partitioned without each piece being meaningless in isolation, or when integration across pieces is so tight that teaching the parts separately produces false understanding.

inputs.name: Topic partitionable into N comparable pieces
inputs.description: A subject area that can be divided into sections of roughly equal depth and importance — each piece substantial enough to require real study, and each essential to understanding the whole. A topic that partitions into one hard piece and several thin ones produces expert groups of unequal value.

inputs.name: Groups sized to match the partition
inputs.description: Home groups of N members, one per topic piece, and expert groups of the same size (one member from each home group assigned the same piece). The structure breaks down if group sizes are mismatched to the partition.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has mastered one piece of the topic deeply (through expert group study) and has learned the remaining pieces through peer teaching — more engaged with each piece than passive reading would produce, because they both taught one and were genuinely dependent on others for the rest.

outputs.name: Whole-topic account assembled from peer teaching
outputs.description: The home group's combined understanding of the full topic — the masterpiece — assembled from each member's expert-group mastery and teaching, which is demonstrably richer than what any individual could have produced from reading the whole topic alone.

steps.name: Partition the topic and assign expert pieces
steps.description: Divide the topic into N pieces of comparable depth. Form home groups of N members. Assign each member one piece — the piece they will become the expert on for their home group. Every member of the home group holds a different piece; no two members share the same assignment.

steps.name: Expert group study
steps.description: All members assigned the same piece meet as an expert group to study it together — not just individually. The expert group's job is to understand their piece well enough to teach it to people who have not seen it. Expert groups should discuss, not just read in parallel.

steps.name: Return to home group and teach
steps.description: Each member returns to their home group and teaches their piece to the others. Each member's piece is the only source of that information available to the home group, so the teaching is not a courtesy — it is genuinely necessary. The listener asks questions; the teacher must answer them without the expert group to fall back on.

steps.name: Assess whole-topic understanding
steps.description: Follow up with individual assessment on the whole topic — not just the piece each student taught. This step confirms that the peer teaching produced genuine learning, and it creates accountability for the listening as well as the teaching step.

principles: Structural necessity, not moral exhortation — the jigsaw works because each student's piece is genuinely needed by the others, not because the instructor asks students to cooperate. Design the partition so that cooperation is the rational strategy.

Expert group quality determines home group outcomes — the teaching step in the home group is only as good as the understanding developed in the expert group. The expert group meeting is not optional preparation, it is the learning that enables the teaching.
patterns.threshold: Each home group member can answer questions about pieces they did not study directly — the peer teaching transferred genuine understanding, not just bullet points — demonstrated by individual whole-topic assessment scores.
patterns.masterpiece: Individual assessment results showing that all home group members understand all pieces of the topic — including the pieces they only learned through peer teaching — confirming that the cooperative interdependence produced real learning transfer.

known_uses.name: Elliot Aronson — Austin, Texas, 1971
known_uses.quote: My graduate students and I had invented the jigsaw strategy that year, as a matter of absolute necessity to help defuse an explosive situation.
known_uses.note: Aronson invented jigsaw in 1971 in Austin during school desegregation. The formal publication is Aronson, E., Blaney, N., Stephan, C., Sikes, J., and Snapp, M. (1978). The Jigsaw Classroom. Sage.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of expert groups that spend their time reading individually rather than discussing and building shared understanding. Expert group members who have not truly mastered their piece produce home group teaching sessions that transmit bullet points rather than understanding — the peer teaching mechanism transfers only as deep as the expert group's preparation.

The anti-pattern of partitioning the topic into pieces of unequal depth, so that some expert groups have substantive material to master and teach while others have thin coverage. Unequal pieces undermine the structural necessity that makes jigsaw work: if one piece is trivially easy, its holder is not genuinely essential to the home group's understanding.

The anti-pattern of omitting the individual whole-topic assessment, removing accountability for the listening step. Without individual assessment on the whole topic, learners can free-ride on the teaching step — attending without genuinely learning what is being taught — because there is no cost to passive listening.

sources.title: The Jigsaw Classroom — official site (Elliot Aronson)
sources.quote: My graduate students and I had invented the jigsaw strategy that year, as a matter of absolute necessity to help defuse an explosive situation.

sources.title: The Jigsaw Classroom (Aronson, Blaney, Stephan, Sikes, Snapp, 1978)
sources.quote: Jigsaw is a cooperative learning technique in which students teach part of the regular curriculum to a small group of their peers.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Topic partitioned into N pieces
home groups formed] --> B[Each member assigned
one piece as expert topic]
    B --> C[Expert groups meet
all holders of same piece study together]
    C --> D[Expert group mastery
deep understanding of their piece]
    D --> E[Return to home group
each member teaches their piece]
    E --> F[Home group assembles
whole-topic understanding]
    F --> G[Individual whole-topic assessment
confirms peer teaching transferred]
    G --> H[More capable learner
deep on own piece, solid on all others]

patterns.example_scenario: A high school history class is studying the causes of the First World War. The teacher divides the topic into four pieces: nationalism, the alliance system, imperial rivalry, and the assassination at Sarajevo. Home groups of four are formed. Each member is assigned one cause to become expert on. The four students assigned 'the alliance system' meet as an expert group: they read, discuss, and work through why the alliance structure meant a small conflict could become a continental war — they test each other until they can explain it clearly. They return to their home groups. The student who studied alliances teaches it; the others ask questions; she must answer without the expert group. The student who studied nationalism teaches next. After four rounds, each home group has assembled an account of all four causes. Individual essays show that students understand all four causes — including the ones they did not study directly — more deeply than they would have from reading all four sections alone. The teaching-and-questioning cycle forced genuine understanding at each step.
